title: CLAUDE.md: Layered Context System
concept: CLAUDE.md files are loaded automatically based on your working directory, creating a layered context system so Claude always has the right background without you repeating yourself.
prompt:
I want to set up a CLAUDE.md hierarchy for my projects. Here's my folder structure:

[paste your folder structure]

Create a CLAUDE.md for each level: global rules at ~/.claude/, portfolio map at ~/projects/, domain knowledge at the team folder, and project-specific context at the repo level.
what_to_change: Your actual folder structure
The specific domain knowledge for each team (e.g. 'marketing', 'data', 'growth')
Project-specific tools, agents, and boundaries
example_output: A set of layered CLAUDE.md files where ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md contains working style rules, ~/projects/CLAUDE.md maps your project portfolio, and each team/project folder has its own domain context — so Claude always knows what it's working in.

title: Skills: Encode Complex Workflows as Slash Commands
concept: Skills turn multi-step workflows into simple slash commands. /commit, /datako, /morning — stop re-explaining workflows and start invoking them.
prompt:
I want to build a skill for [describe the workflow you repeat]. Here's what I currently do each time:

[paste your manual steps]

Write a SKILL.md file that encodes this as a slash command I can run with /[skill-name].
what_to_change: The workflow you want to encode
Your manual steps
The skill name
example_output: A SKILL.md file saved to ~/.claude/skills/[name].md that Claude loads and executes with a single /command, replacing a multi-step manual process.

title: /resume and /btw: Session Recovery and Mid-Run Questions
concept: Use /resume to recover a session you exited. Use /btw to ask questions mid-run without interrupting the flow.
prompt: Mid-run example: type /btw then ask 'What step are you on right now and what's the plan for the next 3?' — you'll get a status update without breaking anything.
what_to_change: The question you want answered mid-run
example_output: A status update mid-execution: current step, what's next, any blockers — without stopping the task.

title: Ctrl+O: See What Claude Is Actually Thinking
concept: Ctrl+O opens Claude's extended thinking panel — surfaces what assumptions it's making from your prompt. Use it to debug when outputs feel off.
prompt: Run Ctrl+O after any response that felt off or unexpected. Look for: what problem Claude thinks you're solving, what constraints it assumed, and what it decided to prioritize.
what_to_change: Nothing — this is a keyboard shortcut, not a prompt technique
example_output: A visible reasoning trace showing Claude's interpretation of your prompt — useful for catching mismatches between what you meant and what Claude understood.

title: LLM Fusion Hub: Unlock Claude API for Your Builds
concept: LLM Fusion Hub provides the API tokens needed for Anthropic integration in Sandcastle prototypes. Point your build there instead of managing tokens manually.
prompt: I'm building a Sandcastle prototype and need to integrate Claude. Help me connect to LLM Fusion Hub at developers.a.musta.ch and set up the API token flow for Anthropic/Claude.
what_to_change: Your specific prototype's tech stack
Whether you need streaming, tools, or basic chat
example_output: A working API integration config that uses the shared LLM Fusion Hub token, avoiding the need to manage individual Anthropic API keys.

title: /loop: Automate Recurring Tasks
concept: Use /loop to schedule skills to run automatically — morning summaries to your Slack DM, dashboard refreshes, weekly digests.
prompt:
/loop 8:55am /morning

Or for a recurring query: /loop 9am "Run the weekly campaign performance summary and post to #growth-ai"
what_to_change: The time you want it to run
The skill or task to execute
The destination (Slack channel, DM, etc.)
example_output: An automated routine that runs on schedule without you initiating it — e.g. a morning briefing in your Slack DM every day at 8:55am.

title: Slack Integration: Read + Write + Build a 'You' Agent
concept: Slack is now read/write in Claude. Use it to scrape your communication style and build a 'you' agent, save formats specific users prefer, or run a /morning skill that reviews your Slack mentions and priorities.
prompt: Read the last 30 messages I've sent in Slack. Identify: my typical sentence length, how I open messages, how I close them, my emoji usage, and the level of formality I use with different people. Then summarize my communication style as a profile I can reuse.
what_to_change: Which channels or threads to analyze
Whose messages to study (yours vs. a stakeholder's)
What attributes matter to you (formality, length, structure)
example_output: A communication style profile: 'Josh writes in short punchy sentences, opens with context not pleasantries, uses questions to drive decisions, rarely uses emoji except in casual channels.' Reusable as a persona prefix.

title: Scheduled Routines: Daily Summaries and Auto-Queries
concept: Combine /loop with skills to auto-run daily summaries to Slack DM or recurring dashboard queries — no manual trigger needed.
prompt: Build a routine that runs every Monday at 9am: pull last week's campaign performance from the data warehouse, format it as a 5-bullet summary with the top metric, biggest mover, and one recommendation, then post it to #growth-ai.
what_to_change: The schedule (daily, weekly, specific days)
The data source and query
The format and destination channel
example_output: An automated weekly post in #growth-ai with a structured performance summary — no manual effort after setup.

title: Meeting Prep in 30 Seconds
concept: Paste any doc, deck, or email thread before a meeting and get a cheat sheet: key points, open questions, 3 things to bring up. No setup required.
prompt:
Here's the doc/deck/thread for my meeting in [X minutes]:

[paste content]

Summarize the key points, flag any open questions or unresolved decisions, and give me 3 things I should bring up or push on.
what_to_change: The doc, deck, or thread content
Meeting context if relevant (who's in the room, what decision is being made)
example_output: A 5-bullet cheat sheet: 3 key points, 2 open questions, 3 suggested talking points — readable in under 60 seconds before you walk in.

title: Role-Frame Before You Ask
concept: Assign Claude a specific perspective before asking for critique. 'Skeptical VP who's seen a hundred of these' produces sharper feedback than 'review this.' Works on any deliverable.
prompt:
You're a skeptical VP who's seen a hundred of these. What's missing, what's weak, and what would you push back on?

[paste your brief/email/strategy/proposal]
what_to_change: The role (skeptical VP, experienced operator, target customer, journalist, competitor)
The deliverable being reviewed
What type of feedback you need (gaps, risks, pushback, tone)
example_output: Direct, specific critique: 'The success metric is undefined — a VP will ask what 'improved engagement' means in numbers. The timeline has no buffer. The recommendation buries the ask in paragraph 3.' Not polite agreement.

title: Force the Final Deliverable
concept: Stop using Claude as a drafting assistant. Force it to produce the final, shareable deliverable directly — ready to send, not requiring a rewrite.
prompt:
Give me a one-pager a CMO could read in 90 seconds and make a go/no-go call on. No fluff, no caveats. Lead with the recommendation. Here's the context:

[paste context]
what_to_change: The audience and their time constraint
The decision they need to make
The format (one-pager, exec summary, Slack message, email)
example_output: A single-page document with the recommendation at the top, 3 supporting points, one risk callout, and a clear ask — formatted to be forwarded without edits.

title: Ask What You Should Be Asking
concept: Before strategic work, ask Claude what question you're not asking. Break your own framing before you commit to it.
prompt:
Here's the situation:

[paste context — brief, strategy, problem statement]

What's the most important question I'm probably not asking that I should be asking before I proceed?
what_to_change: Your actual situation/context
Optionally specify the domain: 'in terms of risk', 'from a customer perspective', 'about the data'
example_output: 'You haven't asked who owns this decision when it goes wrong. You're designing for success but there's no accountability model for failure.' — a reframe you wouldn't have gotten by asking Claude to validate your plan.

title: Pre-Mortem: Ask for the Failure Story
concept: Don't ask 'what could go wrong' — you get a generic list. Ask Claude to write the post-mortem. Narrative format forces specificity.
prompt:
It's 6 months from now and this failed badly. Walk me through what happened — the specific sequence of events, decisions that didn't land, and signals we missed.

[paste your plan/campaign/strategy]

Then ask: Which of these could we have seen coming?
what_to_change: The plan, campaign, or strategy being evaluated
Optionally specify the failure mode: 'focus on adoption failures', 'focus on execution risks'
example_output: A narrative failure story: 'By month 2, the audience size had shrunk 40% due to unaddressed suppression logic. The team didn't catch it because there was no metric tracking list health. By month 4, deliverability had dropped...' — specific, sequenced, and actionable.

title: Split Complex Asks Into Steps
concept: Never pack multi-part tasks into one prompt. Ask the model to plan first, then execute each step. Each call is sharper because the previous output grounds it.
prompt:
Step 1 — Planning:
"Here's the task: [describe complex task]. Before doing anything, write out a step-by-step plan. List each step, what input it needs, and what it produces. Don't execute yet."

Step 2 — Execute:
"Now run Step 1: [step name]. Here's the input: [paste]"

Repeat for each step.
what_to_change: Your complex task description
The inputs for each step as you execute
example_output: A plan with 4-6 discrete steps, each with clear inputs and outputs — then sharp, focused execution on each step rather than a sprawling one-shot attempt that tries to do everything at once.

title: Context First: What You Put In Determines What You Get Out
concept: AI doesn't know your situation unless you tell it. Add 2-3 sentences of background before any question — who it's for, what you're trying to do, what constraints matter.
prompt:
Context: I'm [role] working on [project]. The audience is [who], the goal is [outcome], and the constraint is [time/format/tone].

Now: [your actual question or task]
what_to_change: Your role and project
The audience
The goal and the specific constraint that matters most
example_output: Output tailored to your actual situation instead of a generic answer. The more specific the context, the more specific the output — 'a one-paragraph Slack update for a non-technical stakeholder who only cares about the decision' vs. 'a summary.'

title: Session Distillation: Write the Prompt for Your Next Session
concept: Long sessions degrade. Outputs get softer and more generic after 30+ messages. End every productive session with a distilled prompt that lets you resume at full quality.
prompt:
Before you close a session:
"Summarize everything we've established — my goal, the constraints, the decisions we made, and any context I'd need to resume this cold. Write it as a single prompt I can paste at the start of a new session to pick up exactly where we left off."
what_to_change: Nothing — use this verbatim at the end of any valuable session
example_output: A single copy-pasteable block: 'You are helping me [goal]. Key constraints: [list]. Decisions made so far: [list]. Context needed: [background]. Pick up from: [current state].' Paste into a new session for full performance.

title: Agent Stop Conditions: Prevent Confident Hallucination
concept: Agents without stop conditions hallucinate confidently. Add one line to every agent prompt: if you can't find the data after 3 attempts, stop and return a failure summary.
prompt:
Add this to every agent prompt:

"If you cannot find the required data after 3 attempts, or if a tool is unavailable, STOP immediately and return a failure summary — do NOT keep retrying or filling in assumptions. A clear error message is more valuable than a wrong answer."

And add this to your design process:
"What does failure look like for this agent?" before "What does success look like?"
what_to_change: The number of attempts (3 is usually right)
What 'required data' means for your specific workflow
example_output: When the agent can't find what it needs, it returns: 'BLOCKED — could not find [X] after 3 attempts. Last tool result: [Y]. Suggest: [alternative approach].' Instead of a confident wrong answer delivered at 3am.
